original_abstract: For nearly a century, the Fischer-Tropsch (FT) process has remained a subject of rigorous scientific debate, particularly regarding the fundamental nature of its active sites and reaction mechanisms on cobalt and iron catalysts. Over the past fifteen years, the field has undergone a paradigm shift driven by a "bottom-up" approach that integrates surface science with molecular modeling. Theoretical frameworks have provided a clearer structural profile of cobalt catalyst particles, while recent surface science experiments and density functional theory (DFT) calculations have underscored how realistic surface coverages can trigger surface reconstructions and dictate the stability of reaction intermediates. For cobalt-based systems, a consensus on the active sites and mechanism is finally emerging through the convergence of detailed microkinetic simulations and mechanistic experiments. In contrast, iron-based catalysts present a greater challenge; their dynamic phase evolution under reaction conditions complicates the identification of active surface structures. While emerging techniques are beginning to address this combinatorial complexity, the lack of a definitive structural model for iron active sites continues to hinder a unified mechanistic understanding. Finally, exploring the direct hydrogenation of CO₂ into long-chain hydrocarbons offers a promising and sustainable frontier for the future of Fischer-Tropsch synthesis.
humanized_abstract: Fischer-Tropsch (FT) synthesis—a process that has been used commercially for almost a century to convert syngas into hydrocarbons—is a process that remains at the center of a great deal of debate in terms of the structure of active sites and mechanisms that occur over cobalt and iron catalysts. In the last 15 years or so, however, the field has moved in a completely different direction using what has been termed a "bottom-up" approach, which has combined surface science with computer simulations, and has provided a detailed structural model of Co Fischer-Tropsch catalysts. In combination with detailed surface science studies and density functional theory (DFT) calculations of the influence of realistic catalytic surface coverages, the FT synthesis mechanism and the identification of the active sites on Co catalysts has, for the first time, arrived at a consensus for Co-based catalysts using detailed microkinetic modeling and mechanistic studies. It is not the case, however, that the same level of knowledge has been achieved for iron-based FT catalysts, owing to the dynamic phase changes that occur at their surfaces during FT synthesis. New technologies are beginning to investigate the complex relationships that exist between the many facets of iron FT catalysts, and the search for the "correct" structural model of the active iron sites continues. Ultimately, however, FT synthesis is in need of a new mission, namely to facilitate the conversion of CO2 into higher hydrocarbons, in particular longer hydrocarbons, directly through hydrogenation.
